citation: Han Z, Sun P, Chen T, Dong Y. Club-based vs. hospital-guided rehabilitation after ACL reconstruction in football participants: a two-year prospective cohort study. Front Sports Act Living. 2025;7:1641750. doi: 10.3389/fspor.2025.1641750
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared two-year clinical outcomes of club-based versus hospital-guided rehabilitation in 40 football participants following primary ACL reconstruction. The club-based group (n=20) received intensive sport-specific training at a football base, while the hospital-guided group (n=20) underwent standardized function-oriented rehabilitation at a sports medicine center. Outcomes included subjective functional scores (Tegner, Marx, IKDC, Lysholm, ACL-RSI), objective knee function, return-to-sport timing, and re-injury rates over a two-year follow-up.
tldr: Football players recovering from ACL reconstruction achieve meaningfully better outcomes with club-based sport-specific rehabilitation compared to standard hospital-based care. At two years, club-based athletes scored significantly higher on activity scales (Tegner: 8.5 vs. 7.2) and psychological readiness (ACL-RSI: 73.3 vs. 54.3), returned to recreational matches ~6 weeks earlier (12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks), and returned to official competition ~7.5 weeks earlier (19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks) — all without increasing re-injury risk. Competitive RTS rate was 90% vs. 65%. Football programs should integrate high-intensity sport-specific rehabilitation (agility, cutting, simulated contact) directly into post-ACL recovery protocols.
methods: Prospective cohort design; 40 football players and enthusiasts aged 18-40 who underwent primary ACL reconstruction at Huashan Hospital, Fudan University (2018-2021). Hospital group: standardized physical therapy at the Sports Medicine Center emphasizing ROM, strength, pain control. Club group: sport-specific program at Dalian Football Base (Coach Peng Sun) including agility, explosive power, change-of-direction maneuvers, and match simulation with twice-daily sessions. Assessment: preoperative baseline and 2-year postoperative follow-up. Instruments: Tegner Activity Score, Marx Activity Rating Scale, IKDC, Lysholm, ACL-RSI, joint effusion, Lachman/anterior drawer/lever tests, ROM. Return-to-sport timing recorded. Statistical analysis: independent t-tests or Mann-Whitney U for continuous variables; chi-square for categorical variables.
findings: Club-based group showed significantly higher Tegner score (8.5±1.4 vs. 7.2±1.0, p=0.006), Marx score (12.7±2.8 vs. 10.6±2.7, p=0.019), and ACL-RSI (73.3±21.4 vs. 54.3±18.7, p=0.003). No significant differences in IKDC (90.0 vs. 91.5) or Lysholm (93.2 vs. 90.2) scores. Competitive RTS rate: 90% (club) vs. 65% (hospital). Return to recreational match: 12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks (p=0.042); return to official match: 19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks (p=0.002). No significant differences in ROM, joint stability, joint effusion, or re-injury rate. One reinjury per group occurred.
limitations: Non-randomized prospective cohort with residual confounding despite baseline matching; professional players more prevalent in club group (55% vs. 30%, non-significant) potentially introducing motivation/fitness bias; both artificial ligament and autologous tendon grafts included in same analysis; club-based rehab requires substantially higher time, resource, and athlete commitment limiting accessibility; only two assessment time points (baseline and 2 years) with no intermediate follow-up; small sample size (n=20 per group).
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs should embed sport-specific rehabilitation within their own facilities alongside sports medicine oversight rather than relying exclusively on clinic-based care. Club-based rehab significantly accelerates return to competitive play and improves psychological readiness without increasing re-injury risk. Programs should include agility drills, explosive training, change-of-direction work, and simulated contact scenarios beginning in mid-phase rehabilitation (4-6 months post-surgery).
athleteDev: Football athletes post-ACL reconstruction benefit from early integration of football-specific movement patterns — including 5-10-5 shuttles, slalom runs, targeted passing drills, and small-sided game simulations — within the rehabilitation timeline. This approach preserves and develops neuromuscular adaptations specific to football demands and accelerates return to full competitive readiness compared to generic clinical rehabilitation.
rtp: Club-based rehabilitation enables significantly faster return to play: recreational match participation at ~12 weeks and official competition at ~19 weeks post-ACL reconstruction versus 18 and 26.5 weeks with hospital-guided care. Competitive RTS rate at 2 years was 90% vs. 65%. ACL-RSI psychological readiness was markedly superior in the club group, a well-established predictor of successful RTS. Re-injury rates were equivalent (one per group), supporting the safety of the accelerated sport-specific approach.

citation: Eichner ER. College Football: Conditioning Them to Death; Splenic Infarction: Keeping it in the Family. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2018;17(9):278-279.
abstract: This editorial commentary by Dr. E. Randy Eichner reviews the epidemiology of non-traumatic college football deaths since 2000, identifying that 30 of 32 such deaths occurred during conditioning sessions rather than practice or gameplay. The 30 conditioning deaths are attributed to exertional sickling associated with sickle cell trait (ECAST, n=12), cardiac disease (n=10), exertional heat stroke (EHS, n=7), and asthma (n=1). The piece also presents two published family case reports of altitude-induced splenic infarction in individuals with sickle cell trait.
tldr: Thirty non-traumatic college football deaths since 2000 have occurred during conditioning sessions — not games or practice — driven by exertional sickling (12), cardiac disease (10), exertional heat stroke (7), and asthma (1). The common theme is 'too much, too fast, too soon, too long.' The NCAA's 2010 SCT screening program reduced ECAST deaths from 10 in the prior decade to 1 in 8 years of the program. EHS is preventable with sound conditioning practices. Football programs must adopt scientifically based, progressively loaded, appropriately monitored conditioning protocols — and coaches who enforce reckless sessions must be held accountable.
methods: Narrative editorial/commentary. Epidemiological data sourced from Scott Anderson (head AT, University of Oklahoma), who tracked college non-traumatic football deaths from 2000 onward and published in Journal of Athletic Training (2017). Second section presents two previously published case reports of altitude-related splenic sickling in father-son pairs with sickle cell trait (West J Med 1985; Int J Appl Basic Med Res 2017).
findings: 30 of 32 college non-traumatic football deaths (since 2000) occurred during conditioning, comprising 12 ECAST, 10 cardiac, 7 EHS, 1 asthma. NCAA SCT screening program (initiated 2010) cut ECAST deaths in Division-I football from 10 in the prior decade to 1 in 8 program years. One university admitted negligence in the single ECAST death under the program. EHS deaths continued; a June 2018 lineman death followed 10x110-yard sprints and required emergency liver transplant. Liver transplant has been successfully performed for EHS but is not a reliable cure. Two familial SCT splenic infarction cases presented at altitude (7,200 ft and 12,756 ft); one pair required splenectomy, the other recovered conservatively in 5-10 days.
limitations: Editorial/opinion format without systematic data collection or statistical analysis; cause-of-death classification relies on available reports and may include misclassification (e.g., HCM misclassified as EHS); limited individual conditioning program detail; the cardiac death category may partially overlap with heat-related or sickling mechanisms; external reviews of implicated programs did not reliably prevent recurrence.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches must design sessions that are scientifically based, football-specific, progressively loaded, and appropriately monitored. Pre-participation SCT screening and strict adherence to NCAA SCT guidelines is mandatory and demonstrably saves lives. EHS prevention requires avoiding extreme single-session volumes, especially in heat and early in the conditioning cycle. Athletes with SCT traveling to altitude require counseling about splenic infarction risk. Programs with prior catastrophic events must implement sustained cultural and procedural change beyond external review.
athleteDev: Conditioning programs for college and professional football athletes must apply progressive overload principles and avoid extreme single-session volumes such as mass repetitions of maximum-distance sprints for untested or returning players. Athletes with sickle cell trait require specific intensity and recovery modifications. Early-season and return-from-offseason conditioning sessions carry the highest catastrophic risk and demand the most careful medical oversight and dose escalation.
rtp: Athletes who experience EHS or ECAST require complete medical clearance before return to conditioning or competition. Liver transplant recipients and those with severe multi-organ involvement face protracted and uncertain RTP timelines. Athletes with SCT who suffer splenic infarction can typically return after conservative management (descent, rest, monitoring) but must be educated about recurrence risk at altitude and during high-intensity exercise.

citation: Adlou B, Grace J, Wilburn C, Weimar W. College-to-NFL Stadium Turf Transitions as a Risk Factor for Lower Extremity Non-Contact Injuries in Rookie Players: A 13-Year Cohort Analysis. Healthcare. 2025;13(14):1415. doi: 10.3390/healthcare13121415
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 826 first- and second-round NFL draft picks (2012-2024) to determine whether the transition in home stadium turf type from the final college season to the rookie NFL season influenced lower extremity non-contact injury (LE-NCI) risk. Six transition categories were defined (Grass-to-Grass, Grass-to-Artificial, Artificial-to-Grass, Artificial-to-Artificial, Grass-to-Hybrid, Artificial-to-Hybrid). Competing risk analysis (Aalen-Johansen) and multivariable logistic regression assessed LE-NCI associations with turf transition, position group, college multi-surface training access, and draft cohort.
tldr: College-to-NFL surface transitions do not independently drive lower extremity non-contact injury risk in rookie players — but position does. Skill players (WR, RB, DB, CB, S) had 88% higher adjusted odds of LE-NCI compared to hybrid position players (AOR=1.88; 95% CI: 1.20-2.97; p=0.006) regardless of turf type. Cumulative LE-NCI incidence reached 33.1% by season end, with injury acceleration clustering between weeks 4 and 10. NFL medical staff should concentrate prevention resources on skill position neuromuscular training and heightened monitoring during the weeks 4-10 high-risk window, rather than adjusting surface-transition protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study; 826 NFL first- and second-round draft picks (2012-2024) from Auburn University Sport Biomechanics Lab (one kicker excluded). Players categorized into four position groups: Linemen (n=305, 36.9%), Skill (n=291, 35.2%), Hybrid (n=177, 21.4%), Quarterback (n=53, 6.4%). Home stadium surface classified as natural grass, artificial turf, or hybrid, yielding six college-to-NFL transition types. College multi-surface training access coded as binary variable. Primary outcome: first LE-NCI during rookie season (preseason + regular season), defined as lower extremity injuries occurring without direct player or object contact. Data sourced from Pro-Football-Reference, NFL.com, ESPN.com, CSB Sports. Aalen-Johansen estimator for cumulative incidence; multivariable logistic regression (two models); sensitivity analysis.
findings: 21.2% (175/826) of players sustained a LE-NCI. No surface transition category was significantly associated with LE-NCI versus Grass-to-Grass reference (p=0.175 bivariate). Skill position players had significantly higher LE-NCI odds vs. hybrid players (AOR=1.88; 95% CI: 1.20-2.97; p=0.006). College multi-surface training access was not protective (AOR=0.97; p=0.874). Cumulative LE-NCI incidence reached 33.1% (95% CI: 28.7-37.5%) by week 18, with steepest acceleration between weeks 4 and 10. Skill players had the highest raw LE-NCI rate (30.2%; 88/291); quarterbacks the lowest (13.2%; 7/53). Grass-to-Grass skill players had the highest absolute LE-NCI count (27/70; 38.6%).
limitations: Retrospective design with 32% of injured players lacking definitive injury type documentation; home stadium surface used as proxy for actual playing surface, not capturing practice or away-game surfaces; footwear-surface interactions not measured; climate and surface temperature not captured; hybrid surface transition groups had small sample sizes limiting power; position-by-transition interaction terms could not be tested due to sparse strata; limited to rounds 1-2 draft picks.
practicalImplications: NFL teams should not prioritize surface-transition-based protocols for rookie injury prevention. Instead, resources should concentrate on skill position players through enhanced neuromuscular training targeting cutting mechanics, landing strategies, and proprioceptive conditioning. The weeks 4-10 window represents the highest-risk period and warrants intensified soft tissue screening, modified training loads, and heightened monitoring specifically for skill positions entering regular season play.
athleteDev: The 88% higher LE-NCI risk for skill positions points to the movement demands of those roles — high-speed cutting, acceleration, and deceleration — as primary injury drivers in the NFL rookie year. Rookie development programs should incorporate position-specific neuromuscular training well before and throughout the professional transition, regardless of the home stadium surface. Early-career load management for skill positions should be prioritized.
rtp: The temporal clustering of LE-NCIs between weeks 4 and 10 provides a data-driven window for heightened RTP caution. Rookies returning from early-season LE-NCIs during this high-risk window should be managed conservatively, with objective neuromuscular readiness assessments before full return to practice and play. Cumulative incidence reaching 33.1% by season end underscores the ongoing injury burden throughout the rookie year.

citation: Miller KC, Adams WM. Common body temperature sites provide invalid measures of body core temperature in hyperthermic humans wearing American football uniforms. Temperature. 2021;8(2):166-175. doi: 10.1080/23328940.2020.1829940
abstract: This experimental study evaluated the criterion validity of four common body temperature measurement sites — oral (T-ORL), axillary (T-AXL), aural/tympanic (T-EAR), and forehead liquid crystal monitor (T-FHD) — as surrogates for rectal temperature (T-REC) in 13 men wearing full American football uniform pads (PADS). Participants underwent four sequential phases in a controlled environmental chamber: rest, exercise-induced hyperthermia (T-REC 39.7-39.8°C), cold-water immersion (CWI, 10°C) until all sites read ≤38°C, and 15-minute post-immersion recovery. All four peripheral sites demonstrated unacceptable bias (exceeding ±0.27°C) from T-REC across testing conditions.
tldr: None of the four commonly used non-invasive temperature sites — oral, axillary, tympanic, or forehead strip — are valid for diagnosing or monitoring exertional heat stroke (EHS) in football players wearing full pads. Oral and axillary sites exceeded the clinical validity threshold at every one of 34 measurement time points. Axillary temperature was catastrophically inaccurate during cold-water immersion (mean bias 11.8°C), dropping nearly to water temperature while rectal temperature remained dangerously elevated. Misdiagnosis based on peripheral temperature could result in premature termination of life-saving CWI. Rectal temperature is the only valid and clinically available standard of care for EHS in football.
methods: Controlled laboratory experiment; 13 healthy recreationally active unacclimated men (age 22±2 y; mass 77.5±8.8 kg; height 181.3±5.7 cm) wore full American football PADS in an environmental chamber (38.7±0.8°C, 38.9±2.4% RH). Phases: 10-min rest; exercise (repeated bouts of 2-min running at 90% age-predicted max HR + 2.25-min walking at 4.8 km/h) until T-REC reached 39.7-39.8°C; CWI at 10.0±0.1°C (non-circulating tub) until all sites ≤38°C; 15-min post-immersion recovery. T-REC: rectal thermistor 15 cm past anal sphincter. T-AXL: taped skin thermistor. T-ORL: digital oral thermometer. T-EAR: digital ear thermometer. T-FHD: liquid crystal forehead monitor. Two-way repeated measures ANOVA (site x time); Bland-Altman bias analysis. Validity criterion: ≤±0.27°C bias from T-REC.
findings: T-ORL and T-AXL statistically differed from T-REC and exceeded ±0.27°C bias threshold at all 34 measurement time points (100%). T-FHD differed from T-REC 8 times at rest, 8 times during exercise, 0 during CWI, and twice during recovery (18/34, 53%); exceeded bias threshold 28 times (82%). T-EAR differed from T-REC 5 times at rest, 0 during exercise, 5 during CWI, once during recovery (11/34, 32%); exceeded threshold 15 times (44%). During CWI T-AXL showed mean bias of 11.8±5.5°C, severely underestimating T-REC. Overall mean bias: T-AXL 4.7±1.9°C, T-ORL 1.0±0.2°C, T-EAR 0.5±0.2°C, T-FHD -0.3±0.4°C. Limits of agreement ranged from -0.3 to 1.4°C (aural) to -6.8 to 15.9°C (axillary).
limitations: T-AXL measured via taped thermistor rather than a standard clinical digital axillary thermometer, potentially creating a warm microclimate; only one PADS uniform configuration tested (not shell configurations); healthy non-athlete male participants only; small convenience sample (13 completers of 15 enrolled); non-continuous recording of T-ORL, T-EAR, T-FHD during CWI due to device lead times; single testing session per participant.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and physicians covering American football must use rectal temperature as the sole valid tool for diagnosing and monitoring EHS. Oral, axillary, tympanic, and forehead devices must not be used as surrogates in padded football players. Sideline emergency action plans must include rectal temperature measurement capability and must not delay or discontinue cold-water immersion based on peripheral temperature readings. Misdiagnosis of EHS as heat exhaustion based on a falsely low T-ORL or T-AXL reading could be fatal.
athleteDev: American football players are 11 times more likely to develop exertional heat illness than athletes in all other sports combined at the high school level. PADS cover ~75% of body surface area and impair evaporative cooling, elevating core temperature more rapidly during exercise. Heat acclimatization progressions and practice policies should account for the dramatically increased thermal burden of full-equipment training. Preseason is the highest-risk period.
rtp: EHS cannot be confirmed or ruled out, and cold-water immersion cannot be safely terminated, using any temperature measurement other than rectal temperature in football players wearing equipment. T-AXL in particular drops nearly to water temperature within seconds of CWI while T-REC remains elevated, creating a dangerous false impression of adequate cooling. Safe RTP after EHS requires confirmed T-REC ≤38°C, complete medical evaluation, and a graduated heat re-acclimatization protocol before return to full activity.

citation: Romero-Morales C, Berzosa-Rojo A, Di Luca-Calabrese D, Vázquez-González S, Abuín-Porras V, Jaén-Crespo G, García-Sanz F, Pareja-Galeano H. Comparative analysis of patellar tendon, achilles tendon and plantar fascia structure in indoor and outdoor football players: a novel cross-sectional pilot study. Sci Rep. 2024;14:3930. doi: 10.1038/s41598-024-54403-3
abstract: This cross-sectional pilot study used ultrasound imaging (USI) to compare patellar tendon (PT), Achilles tendon (AT), plantar fascia (PF), and Hoffa's fat pad thickness between 15 outdoor soccer players and 15 indoor soccer (futsal) players aged 18-30. All players trained three sessions per week plus one match and had at least 8 years of registered playing experience. The study aimed to determine whether differences in playing surface, field size, and movement demands between outdoor and indoor football modalities produce measurable tendon structural adaptations detectable via USI.
tldr: Outdoor soccer and indoor futsal players show virtually identical patellar tendon, Achilles tendon, and plantar fascia thickness by ultrasound, suggesting the load differences between these two football modalities are not sufficient to drive tendon structural adaptation. The only significant difference was a reduction in left Hoffa's fat pad thickness in outdoor vs. indoor players (1.28 vs. 1.38 cm, p=0.026). For performance and medical staff, tendon thickness alone is not a reliable marker of differential training load between playing surfaces. Clinical evaluation combining symptoms, functional testing, and imaging together is more informative than structural tendon imaging in isolation.
methods: Cross-sectional pilot study (March-May 2023) at Universidad Europea de Madrid, following STROBE criteria. 30 registered male football players aged 18-30: outdoor soccer (n=15) and indoor futsal (n=15). Both groups completed 3 training sessions/week (90 min each) plus 1 match/weekend; all had ≥8 years playing experience. USI performed by a single trained therapist using GE LogiQ P7 (4-14 MHz linear transducer, L612 RS). Measurements: PT longitudinal and transverse thickness (30° knee flexion), Hoffa's fat pad thickness, AT longitudinal and transverse thickness (4 cm from calcaneus, prone), PF thickness (medial calcaneal tubercle, prone) — all bilateral. Statistics: Shapiro-Wilk, Student t-test or Mann-Whitney U, Levene's test, stepwise linear regression. SPSS v22.0.
findings: No significant differences in PT-L (p=0.639 R, p=0.795 L), PT-T (p=0.847 R, p=0.629 L), AT-L (p=0.699 R, p=0.560 L), AT-T (p=0.979 R, p=0.653 L), or PF (p=0.929 R, p=0.926 L) between outdoor and indoor groups. PT, AT, and PF showed only 0.01 cm mean difference between groups. Left Hoffa's fat pad was significantly lower in outdoor vs. indoor players (1.28±0.13 vs. 1.38±0.10 cm; p=0.026); right side was non-significant (1.34±0.11 vs. 1.36±0.06 cm; p=0.545). Stepwise linear regression showed left Hoffa's fat pad difference was significant (p=0.048; R²=0.137); dominant leg did not predict the difference. Groups differed in age (outdoor 22.5±1.2 vs. indoor 24.3±2.4 years; p=0.021) but not height, weight, or BMI.
limitations: Small pilot sample (n=15 per group) limiting statistical power; only healthy participants (no tendinopathy cases); dynamic USI not performed; left vs. right sides compared rather than dominant vs. non-dominant limb; exact years of sport practice not recorded; minor competition calendar differences between groups; age differed significantly between groups (potential confounder); Doppler and elastography not included; results should be interpreted with caution pending larger studies.
practicalImplications: Ultrasound-based tendon thickness is not a useful tool for differentiating training load exposure between football players across surface types. Hoffa's fat pad monitoring via USI may have value as an adjunct knee health screening tool given its sensitivity to load differences. Football medical staff should rely on comprehensive clinical evaluation — combining symptoms, functional testing, and imaging — rather than structural tendon thickness alone when assessing lower extremity injury risk or making load management decisions.
athleteDev: The absence of tendon structural differences between outdoor and indoor football players suggests that standard football training volumes across both modalities do not generate sufficient load to drive PT, AT, or PF structural adaptation. Targeted high-load training interventions (e.g., heavy slow resistance training, plyometric progressions) above and beyond normal match and session volumes are likely necessary to produce tendon structural changes that could confer injury protection in football athletes.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play. However, the finding that Hoffa's fat pad was the only structure showing between-group differences is clinically relevant: infrapatellar fat pad pathology can cause anterior knee pain and restrict return to activity. USI assessment of Hoffa's fat pad should be incorporated into anterior knee pain evaluations in football athletes as part of the RTP process, complementing clinical examination and functional testing.
